_meta.note: Built to the Armagh-locked standard. Galway reached an 11th consecutive Connacht final (equalling a county record) but lost it to Roscommon from a winning position; recovered through the All-Ireland series to the quarter-final before a late collapse against Dublin.
_meta.verification: All scorelines, venues and referees verified against primary match reports (gaa.ie, RTE, the42, Irish Examiner, Dublin GAA, Galway GAA) and the official gaa.ie fixtures record. Full scorer lists and two-pointer counts sourced for all four championship games. One scoreline discrepancy noted and resolved (see Westmeath R2A note).

manager.name: Padraic Joyce
manager.note: Long-serving manager. Led Galway to an 11th Connacht final in a row, equalling the county's own records of 1933-43 and 1956-66, but lost the decider to Roscommon. Reworked the team's kickout press between the Connacht final and the All-Ireland series after a restart malfunction cost them in the final.

source.snippet: After much work on short kick-outs following the Connacht final malfunction, Fionn McDonagh getting his hand to Jason Daly's restart and sending Shane Walsh clear for an early goal was a reward for the effort invested.

league.note: Division 1 side. Exact final table position and round-by-round results not yet sourced — flagged as a gap. (Context elsewhere: a March league meeting with Dublin produced a sideline incident that earned Dublin manager Ger Brennan a 12-week suspension.)

source.snippet: He'd been handed a stout 12-week suspension in March following a donnybrook between Dublin and Galway during a National League match.
All-Ireland SFC Quarter-Final Dublin 1-25 Galway 1-21... Galway, down to 14 for the dying embers, battled, but Dublin wouldn't be denied.

results.note: Galway entered at the semi-final stage (as 2025 Connacht champions they were seeded). Full scorer list not yet sourced; Leitrim's file will carry the opposing perspective. Venue per the official fixtures record; some sources list this tie at MacHale Park.

results.venue: King & Moffatt Dr. Hyde Park, Roscommon

scorers.note: 2 two-pointers from play, 1 two-point free, 1 '45; exceptional display
substitute; 61st-minute goal that put Galway six up

source.snippet: Shane Walsh nailed a two-pointer into the wind, and when two Roscommon men jumped for a kickout and the resultant breakaway saw Damien Comer hammer the ball into the roof of the net to make it 2-22 to 3-13 on 61 minutes, it looked like it was all over.

results.note: Led by nine (2-22 to 3-13) with nine minutes left after Comer's goal, then failed to score again as Roscommon kicked eight unanswered points. Shane Walsh's last-second equaliser attempt drifted a yard wide. Galway's bid for a fifth straight Connacht title ended; the 11th consecutive final appearance equalled a county record. Roscommon's two-pointer count (4) read from the same report. Referee: Sean Hurson.

source.snippet: Galway left the sun in Salthill armed with a win and a direct pass into the All-Ireland SFC Quarter-Finals... the gap was nine, 3-20 to 2-17, but a Jack Duncan goal either side of two pointers from Robbie Forde and Sam McCartan ensured Westmeath were back in the reckoning.

results.note: Won in front of a Pearse Stadium sell-out (~25,000), the venue's first full house since 2003. A Shane Walsh solo goal on 67 minutes was the decisive score; led by nine before a late Westmeath rally cut it to two. Official fixtures record gives the final score as Galway 3-21 Westmeath 2-21; some live reports rounded to a 3-20-to-2-17 in-game figure, reconciled to the official total here. Westmeath's two-pointer count (4: Wallace, McCartan, Forde, plus one more) read from the same report. Referee: Brendan Griffin (Kerry). Man of the Match: Cein D'Arcy.

source.snippet: Trailing by six in the closing stages in the 55th minute following a John Maher goal, Dublin's collection of emerging and established players thundered back into the contest... Liam Silke was black carded for a foul on Colm Basquel in the 60th minute. O'Callaghan converted the resultant penalty.

results.note: Elimination game. Led by six points (after John Maher's 55th-minute goal) before a Liam Silke black card and a Con O'Callaghan penalty turned the game; Galway, down to 14, were outscored 1-7 to 0-1 in the closing 13 minutes and didn't score after Shane Walsh's late point. Dublin's two-pointer count (4: Scully x2, Small x1, Basquel x1) read from the same report. Referee: David Gough.

collection_meta.collected_by: Claude (research session, not yet hand-verified by Rory)

collection_meta.notes: Galway's season is a study in the new format's cruelty and its second chances: they lost a Connacht final they led by nine, then used the All-Ireland series back-door structure to recover and reach the last eight, only to lose another game they led by six. Shane Walsh, Robert Finnerty, Cein D'Arcy and captain John Maher are the names to carry forward. The Connacht final collapse and the Dublin quarter-final collapse are a natural narrative pairing — a team that kept building winning positions and couldn't close them.
